authoritative_sources.title: Guide to Enterprise Telework, Remote Access, and Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Security
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a primary public authority for U.S. cybersecurity guidance; this publication is directly about enterprise telework, remote access, BYOD, remote access policy, remote access servers, and prerequisite planning.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: request intake, remote-access eligibility, prerequisite validation, policy caveats, and BYOD/telework boundary routing
practical_use_in_pack: minimum tenant facts for remote-user intake
remote access policy and authorization caveats

authoritative_sources.title: Guide to IPsec VPNs
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes this guide specifically for IPsec VPN selection, deployment, gateway-to-gateway and host-to-gateway use, and operational management.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: site-to-site and host-to-gateway IPsec design, configuration review, and operational evidence
practical_use_in_pack: gateway placement and security association evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Guide to SSL VPNs
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes this SSL VPN guide for enterprise remote-access models, client access modes, security considerations, and operational constraints.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: TLS/SSL VPN design, client access mode selection, remote access prerequisite review, and boundary control
practical_use_in_pack: client mode and portal/network extension distinctions

authoritative_sources.title: Security Architecture for the Internet Protocol
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF RFC 4301 is the core public IPsec architecture standard defining traffic selectors, security policy, IP-layer protection, and packet processing behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: IPsec traffic selector, route policy, architecture, and configuration review
practical_use_in_pack: traffic selector and route-policy edge cases
evidence requirements for packet protection assumptions

authoritative_sources.title: IP Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF RFC 4303 defines ESP encapsulation and protection behavior used by IPsec VPN packet forwarding and MTU-sensitive transport.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: packet encapsulation, MTU, fragmentation, packet protection, and evidence-based troubleshooting

authoritative_sources.title: Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF RFC 7296 is the authoritative public IKEv2 standard for authentication, SA negotiation, child SAs, rekeying, notifications, and failure behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: tunnel bring-up, negotiation, rekey, failure isolation, and incident triage

authoritative_sources.title: Negotiation of NAT-Traversal in the IKE
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF RFC 3947 is the public standard for NAT traversal negotiation behavior used by IPsec VPNs across home, carrier, branch, and cloud NAT boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: remote-user edge cases, NAT traversal intake, and connectivity troubleshooting
practical_use_in_pack: branch VPN route and translation evidence

authoritative_sources.title: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF RFC 8446 is the public TLS 1.3 standard, constraining TLS-based VPN control channel, certificate, and protocol assumptions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: TLS-based VPN design, certificate lifecycle, protocol comparison, and source caveats

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a primary public control standard; AC-17 and related controls establish remote access, audit, access control, system communications, and authority evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility, authorization, audit evidence, logging, control caveats, and compliance routing

authoritative_sources.title: What is AWS Client VPN?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the publisher and operator of Client VPN; its documentation defines AWS-specific endpoints, target networks, routes, authorization rules, authentication, logging, and operational constraints.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud remote-user VPN intake, authorization-rule review, route and endpoint evidence, monitoring expectations

authoritative_sources.title: What is AWS Site-to-Site VPN?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS publishes the official Site-to-Site VPN documentation defining AWS tunnel options, customer gateway and virtual private gateway records, transit gateway attachment behavior, and route model.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud site-to-site design, redundancy, tunnel option review, and route evidence
practical_use_in_pack: cloud VPN redundancy and routing caveats

authoritative_sources.title: What is VPN Gateway?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes the official Azure VPN Gateway documentation for site-to-site, point-to-site, VNet-to-VNet, gateway SKUs, routing, and availability behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Azure VPN prerequisite review, topology design, gateway capability validation, and provider caveats

authoritative_sources.title: About Azure Point-to-Site VPN connections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes this specific point-to-site guidance for remote-client VPN architecture, authentication methods, client address pools, tunnel types, and gateway constraints.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: remote-user intake, client configuration, authentication handoff, and authorization prerequisite validation
practical_use_in_pack: client pool and authentication handoff evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Networks and tunnel routing
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud publishes the official Cloud VPN routing documentation for HA VPN, Classic VPN, static and dynamic routing, policy-based routing, traffic selectors, and route maintenance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud VPN routing intake, BGP/static route decisions, traffic selector review, and provider-specific failure modes
practical_use_in_pack: traffic selector and route-policy edge cases
dynamic versus static routing conflict scenario

authoritative_sources.title: WireGuard Protocol
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WireGuard publishes the protocol reference for WireGuard tunnel behavior, cryptographic assumptions, static keys, endpoint roaming, and transport characteristics.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: WireGuard design comparison, key lifecycle, roaming behavior, and troubleshooting caveats

authoritative_sources.title: OpenVPN Protocol
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OpenVPN publishes protocol documentation for OpenVPN-compatible behavior, TLS control channel assumptions, data channel behavior, and deployment caveats.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: OpenVPN-compatible protocol comparison, client profile review, certificate lifecycle, and interoperability caveats

workflow_stages.name: request intake and scope qualification
workflow_stages.description: Capture requested output, tenant, environment, requester role, affected users or sites, VPN type, urgency, intended decision, topology evidence, and whether the work is networking-owned before substantive execution.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: eligibility, authorization, and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate requester authority, tenant namespace, remote-access user group, access owner, topology access, route/DNS/IPAM/log evidence, client profile records, and change or approval owner before design guidance is actionable.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: source and tenant-record selection
workflow_stages.description: Select current IETF, NIST, cloud-provider, protocol, vendor, and tenant-policy sources; record source freshness, decay, source precedence, and conflicts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: in-scope VPN networking guidance
workflow_stages.description: Produce source-backed design, configuration review, troubleshooting, monitoring, failover, and rollout guidance limited to VPN and remote-access networking artifacts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: boundary routing, refusal, and escalation
workflow_stages.description: Route endpoint, identity, application, firewall, SOC, legal, procurement, risk, or execution work before improvised ownership; escalate source conflicts, missing authority, unsupported preview behavior, and regulated-access ambiguity.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: traceable completion
workflow_stages.description: Emit an intake disposition, prerequisite checklist, routing or escalation summary, design or triage artifact, confidence rating, assumptions, and rollback/validation expectations.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: request record, requester role, tenant namespace, target environment, intended output, urgency, and queue state
site diagrams, topology records, subnet plans, traffic classes, route domains, BGP/static route records, SD-WAN records, NAT policy, and firewall path evidence
VPN gateway, client VPN endpoint, concentrator, customer gateway, virtual network gateway, Cloud VPN, target network association, tunnel option, client profile, and tunnel policy records
IPAM, DNS, DHCP, resolver, search suffix, client address pool, route push, split-tunnel, traffic selector, and authorization-rule records
remote-access user group, access owner, identity handoff reference, endpoint posture dependency, certificate authority, certificate/key inventory, revocation, and key rotation records
tunnel state, IKE/IPsec negotiation logs, TLS/OpenVPN logs, WireGuard peer status, handshake latency, packet loss, flow logs, DNS query logs, auth event logs, and monitoring dashboards
incident tickets, change records, runbooks, maintenance windows, rollback plans, risk exceptions, approval matrix, tenant policies, source precedence records, and evidence repositories

intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, design guidance, configuration review notes, incident triage checklists, tradeoff matrices, monitoring blueprints, routing summaries, and refusal notes
systems_of_record: network source of truth, CMDB, topology repository, architecture decision record repository, and evidence store
IPAM, DNS, DHCP, resolver management, route table, BGP, SD-WAN, NAT, firewall, and cloud route systems

VPN gateway and concentrator administration platforms, cloud VPN platforms, AWS Client VPN and Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway, Google Cloud VPN, WireGuard management records, and OpenVPN-compatible service records
certificate authority, PKI, secrets or key-management system, client profile repository, package distribution records, and revocation records
identity provider group mapping and conditional access references only where needed for network authorization handoff evidence
monitoring, logging, SIEM as evidence source, flow log, tunnel state, alerting, incident, on-call, change-management, and runbook systems
risk register, policy repository, compliance evidence store, exception tracker, legal/compliance routing records, and approval matrix

allowed: classify request as accepted, blocked, refused, routed, or escalated
recommend VPN topology, protocol, gateway placement, route policy, traffic selectors, DNS/address assignment, split/full tunnel behavior, failover, monitoring, and validation checkpoints
recommend evidence-backed troubleshooting sequences for tunnel negotiation, NAT traversal, MTU, DNS, certificate/key, roaming, route, and gateway failures
recommend rollout, rollback, tenant adaptation, and materialization constraints without executing production changes
escalate: required site, route, address, DNS, authorization, telemetry, vendor, source precedence, tenant policy, data classification, or approval records are missing
public source, vendor guidance, tenant policy, contractual rule, or jurisdictional requirement conflicts
unsupported preview feature, undocumented vendor behavior, regulated access, privacy, safety, critical-infrastructure, export-control, or emergency-change pressure is present
identity, endpoint, firewall, SOC, legal, procurement, compliance, risk, or production owner authority is required
refuse: application code or frontend/backend troubleshooting unrelated to network transport
endpoint management, MDM, or EDR policy ownership not limited to tunnel transport evidence
cloud identity governance, conditional access, MFA, or authentication policy engine design
firewall signature tuning, DLP policy authoring, SOC investigation, incident closure, legal/compliance signoff, procurement approval, risk acceptance, or credentialed production execution

domain_failure_modes: overlapping CIDRs or ambiguous traffic selectors causing asymmetric routing, blackholing, or unintended reachability
split-tunnel and resolver policy mismatches leaking traffic or breaking private DNS resolution
NAT traversal, carrier-grade NAT, UDP filtering, or path changes causing intermittent remote-user tunnel failure
IKEv2 negotiation, rekey, DPD/keepalive, clock, certificate, or cipher mismatches causing tunnel flapping
ESP/TLS encapsulation overhead, PMTUD failure, or MSS/MTU mismatch causing large-payload failures
return routes, authorization rules, target network associations, or gateway/client profile misalignment causing one-way connectivity
single gateway, region, or ISP path dependency causing availability failure without validated failover
certificate/key rotation or revocation gaps causing broad remote-access outage or unauthorized persistence
weak monitoring, missing logs, or stale telemetry making incident triage and audit evidence unverifiable
tenant records appear complete but miss authorization, data classification, legal/compliance, or change authority constraints
generic VPN best-practice guidance ignores provider-specific routing, traffic selector, client address pool, or authorization-rule behavior
boundary drift into endpoint, identity, application, firewall, SOC, legal, procurement, risk, or production execution ownership

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant-specific regulatory and contractual remote-access requirements
unresolved_ambiguities.description: Public sources define broad remote-access and control expectations, but each tenant's regulatory, contractual, privacy, employee-monitoring, export-control, and critical-infrastructure obligations can materially alter evidence and approval requirements.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant source precedence, data classification, jurisdiction, contractual caveats, and approval owner before actionable recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: vendor preview or undocumented VPN features
unresolved_ambiguities.description: Preview or undocumented features can change routing, NAT traversal, authorization, logging, or client compatibility behavior.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Escalate to human review or vendor-authoritative documentation; use lab validation before production guidance.

unresolved_ambiguities.description: Remote access often depends on identity groups, conditional access, MFA, client posture, and endpoint compliance, but this lane owns only network transport and handoff evidence.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Route identity and endpoint policy ownership to adjacent specialists while retaining group-to-route, client-profile, and tunnel transport evidence.
